name: SDLC Parallel Assessors Mixed Outcomes Pipeline
description: Software-delivery pipeline with two required assessors on the same subject revision for a concrete repository-backed CLI enhancement.
outcome: A repository-backed CLI enhancement is cleared by multiple assessors before successor work can continue.

definition.process_instructions:
Run the workflow through the authored stages `design-brief`, `implementation`, and `release-readiness`. Treat each stage as a distinct checkpoint of evidence and handoff quality: `design-brief` exists to The design is ready; `implementation` exists to The change is implemented and assessed; `release-readiness` exists to Release packaging is complete.
Use these roles deliberately: `mixed-architecture-lead` Produces the technical design for the feature; `mixed-delivery-engineer` Implements the planned change in the repository; `mixed-quality-assessor` Assesses implementation quality and regression risk; `mixed-security-assessor` Assesses security and misuse risk for the same subject; `mixed-release-manager` Packages the implementation after required assessments clear.
Happy path: start with the earliest unfinished stage, dispatch the best matching producing role for that stage, require a full handoff with concrete artifacts or evidence, then move to the next stage only when the current output is usable. When a later stage is primarily evaluative, dispatch the reviewer or assessor roles after the producing role has handed off; in this playbook the `mixed-quality-assessor` and `mixed-security-assessor` both evaluate the same repository-backed subject revision in parallel, and the `mixed-release-manager` should package only after the current revision has cleared both assessor expectations.
Unhappy path: If any role reports missing evidence, partial delivery, request changes, rejection, blocking risk, or an escalation, keep the current subject open, route work back to the most relevant producing role, and require a fresh handoff before trying again. If two decisions conflict, preserve both in history, but route using the newest subject state and the most restrictive still-active finding. Do not skip a stage just because a later role believes it can improvise around incomplete upstream work. This playbook intentionally drives multiple revisions with mixed assessor outcomes; the orchestrator must not simulate future revisions, must preserve exact lineage across revisions, must rerun the correct producing role after each negative decision, and must not let an older pass override a newer failure.
Completion rules: finish only after the last required stage has a final, internally consistent handoff, the final package matches the current subject state, and every role named above has either contributed or been intentionally skipped for a clear reason grounded in the playbook. Stage-specific closure for this playbook: each `design-brief` work item stays open until `mixed-architecture-lead` has either submitted current-subject handoffs that satisfy the `design-brief` goal or been intentionally skipped with a concrete reason grounded in the playbook; each `implementation` work item stays open until `mixed-delivery-engineer`, `mixed-quality-assessor`, and `mixed-security-assessor` have either submitted current-subject handoffs that satisfy the `implementation` goal or been intentionally skipped with a concrete reason grounded in the playbook; each `release-readiness` work item stays open until `mixed-release-manager` has either submitted current-subject handoffs that satisfy the `release-readiness` goal or been intentionally skipped with a concrete reason grounded in the playbook. Do not fabricate approvals, assessments, escalations, or branch changes that the playbook does not actually justify, and do not treat stale findings as permission to advance new work. Guided closure rule: treat the authored flow as best-intent process guidance. Preferred review paths and stage order SHOULD be followed when they remain useful, but the orchestrator MAY waive a preferred step with a recorded reason when closure is still legally possible. Only explicit blockers grounded in the current subject state should keep work open; non-blocking deviations, redundant reviews, and stale advisory findings should be recorded as callouts instead of preventing closure.

columns.label: Planned
columns.description: Work is defined but not yet assigned.

columns.label: Blocked
columns.description: Work is blocked and needs intervention.

columns.label: Done
columns.description: The work item meets its acceptance criteria.

stages.name: implementation
stages.goal: The change is implemented and assessed.
